question_text: The purpose of preparing a Revaluation Account on retirement of a partner is to:
explanation: A Revaluation Account is prepared to record the effect of changes in the value of assets and liabilities at the time of retirement. This ensures the retiring partner gets their fair share of any increase or decrease in asset/liability values. Without revaluation, the retiring partner may be over- or under-compensated.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

options.text: To record only the increase in asset values
To record the effect of changes in value of assets and liabilities
To distribute profits among remaining partners only
To calculate the goodwill of the firm

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation records both increases AND decreases in asset and liability values — not just increases.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking revaluation only covers appreciation.

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation profit/loss is shared among ALL partners (including retiring) in old ratio, not just remaining.
elimination_hints.misconception: Excluding the retiring partner from revaluation.

elimination_hints.hint: Goodwill is a separate calculation. Revaluation deals with tangible assets and liabilities, not intangible goodwill.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing revaluation with goodwill valuation.

question_text: Profit or loss on revaluation of assets and liabilities on retirement is shared among:
explanation: Revaluation profit or loss is shared among ALL partners (including the retiring partner) in their OLD profit-sharing ratio. This is because the change in value occurred during the period when all partners were together, so everyone must share the impact.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

options.text: All partners (including retiring) in old ratio
Remaining partners only in new ratio

elimination_hints.hint: Remaining partners in new ratio is incorrect because the retiring partner also has a claim on asset value changes during their tenure.
elimination_hints.misconception: Excluding the retiring partner from revaluation.

elimination_hints.hint: Gaining ratio is used for goodwill, not for revaluation. Revaluation uses old ratio since all partners contributed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing gaining ratio usage with revaluation distribution.

elimination_hints.hint: The retiring partner alone doesn't bear or receive all revaluation effects. All partners share equally based on old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assigning entire revaluation to the retiring partner.

question_text: On retirement of C, if the value of building increases from ₹5,00,000 to ₹6,50,000, the Revaluation Account is:
explanation: When an asset increases in value, the asset account is debited (to record the higher value) and Revaluation Account is credited (recording a gain). Building A/c Dr. ₹1,50,000, To Revaluation A/c ₹1,50,000. The credit represents a profit on revaluation.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: Debit to Revaluation means a loss. An increase in asset value is a gain — so Revaluation is credited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing the treatment of gains and losses.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹6,50,000 is the new value, not the change. Only the increase (₹1,50,000) is recorded in Revaluation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Recording the full new value instead of the change.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,00,000 is the old value. The revaluation entry records only the difference = ₹1,50,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using old value instead of the difference.

question_text: On retirement of B, stock decreased from ₹80,000 to ₹72,000 and creditors increased from ₹50,000 to ₹55,000. The Revaluation Account shows:
explanation: Stock decrease = ₹80,000 − ₹72,000 = ₹8,000 loss → Revaluation A/c debited. Creditors increase = ₹55,000 − ₹50,000 = ₹5,000. Increase in liability is a loss → Revaluation A/c debited. Total debit to Revaluation = ₹8,000 + ₹5,000 = ₹13,000 (loss).
subtopic: Decrease in asset and increase in liability
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: ₹8,000 accounts only for stock decrease. The creditors increase (₹5,000) is also a loss that must be added.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring liability changes.

elimination_hints.hint: Both items are losses (asset decreased, liability increased). There's no gain here.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating increase in creditors as a profit.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹3,000 = ₹8,000 − ₹5,000. Both are losses — they should be added, not subtracted.
elimination_hints.misconception: Netting a loss against another loss.

question_text: A, B, and C share profits in 3:2:1. On C's retirement, machinery (book value ₹3,00,000) is revalued at ₹2,70,000 and debtors (₹1,20,000) are written down by 5%. The revaluation loss borne by C is:
explanation: Machinery loss = ₹3,00,000 − ₹2,70,000 = ₹30,000. Debtors write-down = 5% × ₹1,20,000 = ₹6,000. Total revaluation loss = ₹36,000. C's share = 1/6 × ₹36,000 = ₹6,000.
subtopic: Retiring partner's share of revaluation loss
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: ₹5,000 = 1/6 × 30,000. This only considers machinery loss, ignoring debtors write-down of ₹6,000.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring one of the revaluation items.
Using wrong share fraction.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹36,000 is the total loss. C bears only 1/6 of this.
elimination_hints.misconception: Assigning entire loss to the retiring partner.

question_text: On retirement, an unrecorded asset of ₹40,000 is discovered. This asset is recorded by:
explanation: An unrecorded asset is one that exists but is not in the books. To bring it on record: Unrecorded Asset A/c is debited (asset comes in) and Revaluation A/c is credited (it represents a gain). This gain is then distributed to all partners in old ratio.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

options.text: Revaluation A/c Dr. → To Asset A/c
Partners' Capital A/c Dr. → To Asset A/c
Asset A/c Dr. → To Revaluation A/c
Asset A/c Dr. → To Partners' Capital A/c (old ratio)

elimination_hints.hint: This reverses the entry — debiting Revaluation means a loss, but discovering an asset is a gain.
elimination_hints.misconception: Reversing debit and credit.

elimination_hints.hint: Partners' capital accounts are not directly debited for asset discovery. The Revaluation A/c acts as the intermediary.
elimination_hints.misconception: Bypassing the Revaluation Account.

elimination_hints.hint: This bypasses Revaluation A/c. While the ultimate effect on capitals is the same, the standard procedure routes it through Revaluation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Directly crediting partners without using Revaluation A/c.

question_text: On retirement, an unrecorded liability of ₹25,000 is discovered. The Revaluation Account is:
explanation: An unrecorded liability means the firm owes more than shown in books. Recording it: Revaluation A/c is debited (it's a loss — the firm has an additional obligation) and the Liability A/c is credited. This loss is shared by all partners in old ratio.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: Credit to Revaluation means a gain. But an unrecorded liability is an additional cost/obligation — it's a loss.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a liability as a gain.

elimination_hints.hint: Unrecorded liabilities must be recorded to ensure the retiring partner's settlement is fair.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring unrecorded items.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹12,500 is half the amount. The full ₹25,000 is recorded in Revaluation — sharing happens when transferring to capital accounts.
elimination_hints.misconception: Pre-dividing the amount.

question_text: A, B, and C share profits in 5:3:2. C retires. Revaluation shows: Land increased by ₹50,000, Plant decreased by ₹20,000, Creditors decreased by ₹5,000, Provision for doubtful debts increased by ₹8,000. The net revaluation profit/loss is:
explanation:
Gains (credit Revaluation): Land increase = ₹50,000, Creditors decrease = ₹5,000. Total gains = ₹55,000.
Losses (debit Revaluation): Plant decrease = ₹20,000, Provision increase = ₹8,000. Total losses = ₹28,000.
Net = ₹55,000 − ₹28,000 = ₹27,000 profit.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: ₹55,000 counts only the gains. You must subtract the losses (₹28,000) to find net effect.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring losses.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹28,000 counts only the losses. You must net the gains (₹55,000) against them.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring gains.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹22,000 likely comes from an error in classification. Check: creditors decrease is a gain (liability reduced), not a loss.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating creditors decrease as a loss.

question_text: When a liability decreases on revaluation, the Revaluation Account is:
explanation: A decrease in liability means the firm owes less than previously recorded. This is a gain for the firm. Entry: Liability A/c Dr. (reduced), To Revaluation A/c Cr. (gain recorded). So Revaluation is credited.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: Debit to Revaluation = loss. But a decrease in liability is beneficial — it's a gain, so credit.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating liability decrease as a loss.

elimination_hints.hint: Every change in asset/liability value is recorded through Revaluation. It is always affected.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking some changes bypass Revaluation.

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation A/c is closed only after all items are recorded and the net balance is transferred to partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Closing Revaluation prematurely.

question_text: A, B, and C share profits in 2:2:1. C retires. General Reserve in the Balance Sheet is ₹50,000. The amount credited to C's Capital Account from General Reserve is:
explanation: General Reserve is an accumulated profit belonging to all partners in their old ratio. On retirement, it is distributed to all partners in old ratio. C's share = 1/5 × ₹50,000 = ₹10,000. Entry: General Reserve A/c Dr. 50,000, To A's Capital A/c 20,000, To B's Capital A/c 20,000, To C's Capital A/c 10,000.
subtopic: Treatment of General Reserve on retirement
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: ₹25,000 = 1/2 × 50,000. C's share is 1/5, not 1/2. Check ratio: 2:2:1 = total 5, C = 1.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using wrong fraction.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹50,000 is the total reserve. C gets only 1/5 of it.
elimination_hints.misconception: Giving entire reserve to the retiring partner.
Using A/B's fraction for C.

question_text: On retirement of a partner, the debit balance of Profit & Loss Account (accumulated loss) shown in the Balance Sheet is:
explanation: A debit balance in P&L Account represents accumulated losses. On retirement, this must be written off by debiting all partners' capital accounts in old ratio. Entry: All Partners' Capital A/c Dr. (old ratio), To P&L A/c. This ensures the retiring partner bears their share of accumulated losses before settlement.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

options.text: Credited to all partners in old ratio
Debited to remaining partners in new ratio
Debited only to the retiring partner
Debited to all partners in old ratio

elimination_hints.hint: Credit means a gain. An accumulated loss is negative — it debits (reduces) partners' capitals.
elimination_hints.misconception: Treating a loss as a profit.

elimination_hints.hint: Accumulated loss belongs to the period when all partners were present. All must share it in old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Excluding the retiring partner from losses.

elimination_hints.hint: The loss was incurred during all partners' tenure. All share it proportionally, not just the retiring partner.
elimination_hints.misconception: Burdening only the retiring partner.

question_text: Revaluation profit on retirement is shared only by the remaining partners in their new ratio.
explanation: FALSE. Revaluation profit is shared by ALL partners (including the retiring partner) in their OLD profit-sharing ratio. The retiring partner has equal claim on value changes that occurred during the partnership. Excluding them would be unfair.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: Think about fairness — the retiring partner was part of the firm when these assets gained value. They deserve their share.
elimination_hints.misconception: Excluding the retiring partner from revaluation gains.

question_text: An increase in the value of a liability on revaluation results in a credit to the Revaluation Account.
explanation: FALSE. An increase in liability is unfavorable (the firm owes more). It is a loss. Entry: Revaluation A/c Dr. (loss), To Liability A/c Cr. (increased value). The Revaluation Account is debited, not credited.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: Credit to Revaluation = gain. But paying more (increased liability) is not a gain — it's a loss. So Revaluation is debited.
elimination_hints.misconception: Confusing liability increase with a gain.

question_text: Revaluation profit or loss is transferred to all partners' capital accounts in their _______ ratio.
explanation: Revaluation profit or loss is transferred to ALL partners' capital accounts in their OLD (existing) ratio. This is because the changes in value occurred during the period they were partners together.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

question_text: On retirement, accumulated reserves like General Reserve and credit balance of P&L Account are distributed to _______ partners in _______ ratio.
explanation: Accumulated reserves belong to all partners (they were earned during the partnership of all). On retirement, they are distributed to ALL partners (including the retiring one) in their OLD ratio. This ensures the retiring partner gets their rightful share.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

question_text:
Assertion (A): On retirement, revaluation profit/loss is distributed to all partners including the retiring partner.
Reason (R): The change in value of assets and liabilities occurred during the period when all partners were together.

Choose the correct option:
explanation: Both A and R are true, and R correctly explains A. The asset value changes happened while all partners were working together. Since they all contributed to and were affected by these changes, all must share the resulting profit or loss — including the retiring partner.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

options.text: Both A and R are true, and R is the correct explanation of A
Both A and R are true, but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is true but R is false
A is false but R is true

elimination_hints.hint: R is precisely the reason why A is true — the timing of value changes is what justifies including the retiring partner.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not seeing the causal connection between timing and entitlement.

elimination_hints.hint: R is factually true — value changes did occur during the period all partners were together.
elimination_hints.misconception: Doubting a factual statement about timing.

elimination_hints.hint: A is a standard accounting rule — all partners share revaluation effects on retirement.
elimination_hints.misconception: Thinking revaluation excludes the retiring partner.

question_text: A, B, and C share profits in 3:2:1. C retires. The following revaluation and reserve adjustments are made: Revaluation profit ₹24,000; General Reserve ₹60,000; P&L Account (Cr.) ₹18,000; Workmen Compensation Reserve ₹30,000 (no claim exists). What is the total amount credited to C's Capital Account from all these adjustments?
explanation:
All items are distributed to ALL partners in old ratio 3:2:1. C's share = 1/6.
Revaluation profit: 1/6 × 24,000 = ₹4,000
General Reserve: 1/6 × 60,000 = ₹10,000
P&L Cr. balance: 1/6 × 18,000 = ₹3,000
Workmen Compensation Reserve (no claim): 1/6 × 30,000 = ₹5,000
Total credited to C = ₹4,000 + ₹10,000 + ₹3,000 + ₹5,000 = ₹22,000.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: ₹14,000 likely includes only 2 items. All 4 items (Revaluation, General Reserve, P&L, WCR) must be included.
elimination_hints.misconception: Missing some reserve items.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹17,000 likely excludes WCR. Since no claim exists, the entire WCR is distributed to partners.
elimination_hints.misconception: Not distributing WCR when there's no claim.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹19,000 likely excludes P&L credit balance. The P&L credit balance is also an accumulated profit to be distributed.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring P&L credit balance.

question_text: A, B, and C share profits in 4:3:3. B retires. Workmen Compensation Reserve is ₹80,000 and the claim against it is ₹50,000. The surplus reserve distributed to B is:
explanation: Workmen Compensation Reserve = ₹80,000. Claim = ₹50,000. The claim is first met from the reserve: Workmen Compensation Reserve A/c Dr. 50,000, To Workmen Compensation Claim A/c Cr. 50,000. Surplus = ₹80,000 − ₹50,000 = ₹30,000. This surplus is distributed to all partners in old ratio. B's share = 3/10 × ₹30,000 = ₹9,000.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: ₹24,000 = 3/10 × 80,000. This uses the full reserve, but ₹50,000 must first be used for the claim.
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring the claim and distributing full reserve.
Using wrong share fraction.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹8,000 = 1/10 × 80,000. This is neither the correct fraction nor the correct base.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using wrong both fraction and base.

question_text: On B's retirement from A, B, C (ratio 2:3:5), the following adjustments are made: Building appreciated ₹40,000, Stock depreciated ₹15,000, Outstanding expenses ₹5,000 to be recorded, General Reserve ₹1,00,000. B's total credit from revaluation and reserves is:
explanation: Revaluation: Building up = +₹40,000 (gain). Stock down = −₹15,000 (loss). Outstanding expenses (unrecorded liability) = −₹5,000 (loss). Net revaluation profit = 40,000 − 15,000 − 5,000 = ₹20,000. B's share of revaluation = 3/10 × 20,000 = ₹6,000. General Reserve: B's share = 3/10 × 1,00,000 = ₹30,000. Total credit to B = ₹6,000 + ₹30,000 = ₹36,000.
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: ₹30,000 is only B's share of General Reserve. You must also add B's share of revaluation profit (₹6,000).
elimination_hints.misconception: Ignoring revaluation effect.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹42,000 = 3/10 × (40,000 + 1,00,000). This ignores the losses (stock ₹15,000 + outstanding ₹5,000).
elimination_hints.misconception: Including only gains, not losses, in revaluation.

elimination_hints.hint: ₹18,000 likely uses wrong ratio or includes only partial items.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using wrong share or missing items.

question_text:
Match the following adjustments on retirement with the correct ratio used for distribution:

| Column I (Adjustment) | Column II (Ratio used) |
|---|---|
| (i) Revaluation profit/loss | (p) Gaining ratio |
| (ii) General Reserve / P&L balance | (q) Old ratio (all partners) |
| (iii) Goodwill to retiring partner (through capitals) | (r) New ratio (remaining partners) |
| (iv) Write-off of raised goodwill | (s) Old ratio (all partners) |

Choose the correct matching:
explanation:
(i) Revaluation profit/loss → distributed to ALL partners in OLD ratio → (q)
(ii) General Reserve / P&L → distributed to ALL partners in OLD ratio → (s)
(iii) Goodwill adjusted through capitals → debited to gaining partners in GAINING ratio → (p)
(iv) Write-off of raised goodwill → debited to remaining partners in NEW ratio → (r)
Correct: (i)→(q), (ii)→(s), (iii)→(p), (iv)→(r)
subject: accountancy
chapter: Accounting for Partnership: Retirement and Death of a Partner

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation profit goes in old ratio (q), not gaining ratio (p). Gaining ratio is for goodwill, not revaluation.
elimination_hints.misconception: Using gaining ratio for revaluation.

elimination_hints.hint: Revaluation in new ratio (r) is wrong — all partners (including retiring) share revaluation in old ratio.
elimination_hints.misconception: Excluding retiring partner from revaluation.

elimination_hints.hint: General Reserve goes to ALL partners in old ratio (s), not remaining in new ratio (r). The retiring partner also gets their share.
elimination_hints.misconception: Excluding retiring partner from reserves.
